questions.question:
Which one of the following shapes can be used to tile (completely cover by repeating) a flat plane, extending to infinity in all directions, without leaving any empty spaces in between them? The copies of the shape used to tile are identical and are not allowed to overlap.

A. circle
B. regular octagon
C. regular pentagon
D. rhombus
questions.to_find: Which shape can tessellate (tile) a flat plane without gaps or overlaps

understand.plain: Tessellation means covering the entire plane using repeated identical copies of a shape with no gaps and no overlaps. Only certain polygons can tile the plane. A shape tiles the plane if its interior angles divide evenly into 360 degrees at every vertex meeting point. Among the given choices, a rhombus (a parallelogram with equal sides) tiles perfectly because its opposite angles are equal and each pair of adjacent angles sums to 180 degrees, allowing rows to fit together seamlessly.

keywords.explain: Covering a plane completely with repeated copies of one or more shapes, leaving no gaps and creating no overlaps
keywords.example: A checkerboard of squares is a classic tessellation

keywords.explain: For a regular polygon with n sides, each interior angle = (n-2)*180/n degrees; tiling requires this angle to divide 360 evenly
keywords.example: Regular hexagon: each angle = 120 degrees, and 360/120 = 3, so three hexagons meet perfectly at each vertex

keywords.explain: A quadrilateral with all four sides equal; it is a parallelogram, so opposite angles are equal and adjacent angles are supplementary (sum to 180 degrees)
keywords.example: A diamond shape on a playing card is roughly rhombus-shaped

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='200' fill='none'/><text x='210' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Rhombus Tessellation</text><polygon points='60,50 100,30 140,50 100,70' class='hi'/><polygon points='100,70 140,50 180,70 140,90' class='hi'/><polygon points='140,50 180,30 220,50 180,70' class='hi'/><polygon points='60,90 100,70 140,90 100,110' class='bg'/><polygon points='100,110 140,90 180,110 140,130' class='bg'/><polygon points='140,90 180,70 220,90 180,110' class='bg'/><polygon points='60,130 100,110 140,130 100,150' class='hi'/><polygon points='100,150 140,130 180,150 140,170' class='hi'/><polygon points='140,130 180,110 220,130 180,150' class='hi'/><text x='260' y='55' class='sub ink'>Each rhombus</text><text x='260' y='73' class='sub ink'>angle pair = 180 deg</text><text x='260' y='105' class='sub ink'>Rows interlock</text><text x='260' y='123' class='sub ink'>perfectly</text><text x='260' y='155' class='sub ink'>No gaps,</text><text x='260' y='173' class='sub ink'>no overlaps</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A grid of rhombuses in alternating shaded and unshaded rows, showing perfect tessellation with no gaps

given.aim: Identify which shape among circle, regular octagon, regular pentagon, and rhombus can tile the infinite plane without gaps or overlaps

terms.meaning: A round shape with no straight edges; circles always leave curved gaps when packed together
terms.example: Circular coins on a table always leave small gaps between them
terms.connects: Cannot tile the plane - leaves crescent-shaped gaps at every junction

terms.meaning: An 8-sided regular polygon with each interior angle = 135 degrees; 360/135 is not an integer
terms.example: Stop signs are regular octagons; they leave square gaps when placed together
terms.connects: Does not tile alone - leaves square gaps; needs squares to complete the tiling (semi-regular tessellation only)

terms.meaning: A 5-sided regular polygon with each interior angle = 108 degrees; 360/108 = 3.33, not an integer
terms.example: A soccer ball has pentagonal panels but they are assembled on a sphere, not a flat plane
terms.connects: Cannot tile the plane - angles do not divide 360 evenly

terms.meaning: A parallelogram with all four sides equal; the two pairs of angles sum to 360 degrees perfectly, enabling seamless tiling
terms.example: Diamond-pattern floor tiles are rhombuses that tile perfectly
terms.connects: Tiles the plane perfectly because it is a parallelogram - any parallelogram tessellates

given.plan: Check the interior angle condition for each shape: for regular n-gons, angle = (n-2)*180/n must divide 360 evenly for solo tessellation. For non-regular shapes like rhombus, use the parallelogram property.

steps.title: Check circle
steps.apply: Circles have no straight edges and leave curved (crescent-shaped) gaps when placed side by side. A circle cannot tessellate the plane.

steps.title: Check regular octagon
steps.apply: 360/135 = 2.67, which is not an integer. Regular octagons alone cannot tile the plane without gaps (square holes remain). Option B is eliminated.
steps.note: Octagons + squares form a semi-regular tiling, but octagons alone do not

steps.title: Check regular pentagon
steps.apply: 360/108 = 3.33, not an integer. Regular pentagons cannot tessellate the plane. Option C is eliminated.
steps.note: Some irregular pentagons can tile, but a regular pentagon cannot

steps.title: Check rhombus
steps.formula_raw: parallelogram property: adjacent angles sum to 180 degrees; each vertex where four rhombuses meet has angles a + b + a + b = 360 degrees
steps.apply: A rhombus is a parallelogram. Any parallelogram tessellates the plane by translation (sliding). Place rows of rhombuses where each row is offset by half a rhombus. The four angles meeting at each interior vertex sum to exactly 360 degrees, leaving no gaps. Option D is correct.
steps.note: Any quadrilateral (even irregular) can tessellate the plane, so a rhombus certainly can

questions.formula_note: No standard formula ID applies; the key principle is that any parallelogram (including rhombus) tessellates the plane by the 360-degree angle-sum property at each vertex.

questions.question:
Looking at the surface of a smooth 3-dimensional object from the outside, which one of the following options is TRUE?

A. The surface of the object must be concave everywhere.
B. The surface of the object must be convex everywhere.
C. The surface of the object may be concave in some places and convex in other places.
D. The object can have edges, but no corners.
questions.answer: C. The surface of the object may be concave in some places and convex in other places.
questions.to_find: Which statement about the surface curvature of a smooth 3D object is TRUE

understand.plain: A smooth 3D object can have regions where the surface curves inward (concave, like the inside of a bowl) and regions where it curves outward (convex, like the outside of a ball). There is no requirement for the entire surface to be uniformly concave or convex. A torus (donut shape) is a classic smooth 3D object with both concave and convex regions on its outer surface.

keywords.explain: A surface that curves inward when viewed from outside, like the interior of a bowl or the inner ring of a donut
keywords.example: The inner side of a coffee mug is concave when you look inside

keywords.explain: A surface that curves outward when viewed from outside, bulging toward the viewer
keywords.example: The outside of a sphere or balloon is entirely convex

keywords.explain: A 3D shape with no sharp edges or corners; the surface transitions continuously everywhere
keywords.example: A donut (torus) is smooth - you can slide your finger over it without hitting any sharp edge

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='200' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Torus: Both Concave and Convex Regions</text><ellipse cx='200' cy='110' rx='80' ry='40' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><ellipse cx='200' cy='110' rx='35' ry='18' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><text x='80' y='85' class='tag ok'>Convex</text><text x='80' y='103' class='tag ok'>outer ring</text><text x='268' y='125' class='tag no'>Concave</text><text x='268' y='143' class='tag no'>inner ring</text><line x1='126' y1='100' x2='95' y2='90' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='245' y1='118' x2='268' y2='128' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A torus (donut) shape showing convex outer ring and concave inner ring, illustrating both curvature types on one smooth object

given.aim: Determine the true statement about whether a smooth 3D object's surface must be all-concave, all-convex, or can be mixed

terms.meaning: A three-dimensional shape with a continuous surface that has no sharp edges, corners, or discontinuities
terms.example: A sphere, a torus (donut), an ellipsoid, or a peanut-shaped object are all smooth 3D objects
terms.connects: Smooth implies differentiability of the surface, not any restriction on the sign of curvature

terms.meaning: Part of the surface that curves inward (toward the interior of the object) when seen from outside
terms.example: The inner hole of a donut appears concave when you look at the donut from outside
terms.connects: Concave regions have negative Gaussian curvature (saddle-like) in differential geometry

terms.meaning: Part of the surface that bulges outward away from the interior when seen from outside
terms.example: The outer equator of a donut is convex - it rounds away from the center
terms.connects: Convex regions have positive Gaussian curvature, like the surface of a sphere

terms.meaning: A surface that has both concave and convex parts on the same object
terms.example: A torus has convex outer regions and concave inner regions; a figure-8 shaped solid also has both
terms.connects: Smoothness alone does not force uniform curvature sign

given.plan: Use a counterexample to eliminate options A and B. A torus or saddle surface shows that a smooth 3D object can have both concave and convex regions. Then evaluate option D about edges.

steps.title: Eliminate Option A (all concave)
steps.apply: A sphere is smooth and entirely convex. Therefore it is false that a smooth 3D object MUST be concave everywhere. Option A is FALSE.
steps.note: One counterexample suffices to disprove a universal claim

steps.title: Eliminate Option B (all convex)
steps.apply: A torus (donut shape) is smooth but its inner ring region (viewed from outside) is concave. Therefore a smooth object need not be convex everywhere. Option B is FALSE.
steps.note: The inner equatorial circle of a torus has negative mean curvature

steps.title: Evaluate Option C (mixed curvature allowed)
steps.apply: The torus example confirms that a single smooth 3D object can have concave regions (inner hole) and convex regions (outer surface). There is no geometric law preventing this. Option C is TRUE.
steps.note: Smoothness is about continuity/differentiability, not about the sign of curvature

steps.title: Evaluate Option D (edges but no corners)
steps.apply: The problem specifies a SMOOTH object. A smooth surface by definition has no sharp edges or corners anywhere. Option D is inconsistent with the smooth constraint. Option D is FALSE.
steps.note: Smooth = no edges, no corners; this makes option D self-contradictory

solution.result: C. The surface of the object may be concave in some places and convex in other places.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula applies; the reasoning uses the definition of smooth surfaces and the torus as a geometric counterexample to options A and B.

questions.question:
A cube is to be cut into 8 pieces of equal size and shape. Here, each cut should be straight and it should not stop till it reaches the other end of the cube. The minimum number of such cuts required is

A. 3
B. 4
C. 7
D. 8
questions.to_find: Minimum number of straight through-cuts to divide a cube into 8 equal pieces

understand.plain: We need to cut a cube into 8 equal smaller cubes using straight planar cuts that go all the way through. Each cut divides all existing pieces at that level simultaneously. Cutting a cube into 8 equal cubes is equivalent to halving it along each of the three axes (x, y, z), needing exactly 3 cuts. Unlike sawing each piece individually, a single plane can cut through the entire stack at once, so no rearrangement between cuts is needed.

keywords.meaning: A planar cut that passes completely from one side of the object (or current stack) to the other without stopping
keywords.example: Slicing a loaf of bread straight through from top to bottom with a single knife stroke
keywords.connects: Through-cuts can slice multiple pieces at once if they are all in the path of the cutting plane

keywords.meaning: Halving along each dimension creates 2^k equal parts after k cuts along k different axes
keywords.example: 1 cut along x-axis gives 2 pieces; add 1 along y gives 4; add 1 along z gives 8
keywords.connects: The minimum cuts to get 2^n equal cubes from a cube is n (one per dimension per halving)

keywords.meaning: A single planar cut slices every piece it intersects in one pass, without needing pieces to be separated first
keywords.example: Stacking 4 pieces and cutting them all at once with one horizontal slice
keywords.connects: This is what makes 3 cuts sufficient - no need to reassemble between cuts

class='hi'/><text x='295' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Cut 2 (y)</text><text x='295' y='152' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>4 pieces</text><line x1='315' y1='80' x2='330' y2='80' understand.visual_alt: Four stages showing a cube being halved by 3 successive through-cuts along x, y, and z axes to produce 8 equal mini-cubes

given.aim: Find the minimum number of straight planar through-cuts needed to divide a cube into exactly 8 equal pieces of the same shape

terms.meaning: A 3D solid with 6 equal square faces and equal dimensions along x, y, and z axes
terms.example: A standard die or an ice cube; 8 equal sub-cubes are formed by halving each dimension
terms.connects: Halving all three dimensions gives 2 x 2 x 2 = 8 equal smaller cubes

terms.meaning: A planar slice that goes from one face of the current arrangement to the opposite face without interruption
terms.example: Slicing a block of cheese with a wire cutter that goes all the way through
terms.connects: One through-cut can slice multiple stacked pieces in a single pass

terms.meaning: The fewest number of through-cuts sufficient to achieve the desired result
terms.example: Cutting a square into 4 equal squares needs just 2 cuts (horizontal + vertical through the center)
terms.connects: For a cube into 8 equal cubes, 3 mutually perpendicular cuts through the centers suffice

terms.meaning: k through-cuts along k orthogonal planes can create at most 2^k pieces (one per halving per axis)
terms.example: 1 cut -> 2 pieces, 2 cuts -> 4 pieces, 3 cuts -> 8 pieces
terms.connects: 8 = 2^3 requires exactly 3 cuts when pieces need not be reassembled between cuts

given.plan: Determine that 8 = 2 x 2 x 2 means one cut per axis (x, y, z) suffices. Each cut planes through the entire stack, so no rearrangement is needed. Confirm 3 cuts are enough and that 2 cuts can only give at most 4 pieces, so 3 is truly the minimum.

steps.title: Recognize the target: 8 equal mini-cubes
steps.formula_raw: 8 = 2 x 2 x 2 (halve along x, y, and z once each)
steps.apply: The only way to divide a cube into 8 congruent pieces with the same shape is to halve it along each of the three coordinate axes. This gives 2 layers in x, 2 in y, and 2 in z, producing 8 equal smaller cubes.
steps.note: The pieces must be equal in size AND shape, ruling out asymmetric cuts

steps.title: Count cuts needed
steps.formula_raw: cuts needed = log_2(pieces per axis) x number of axes = 1 x 3 = 3
steps.apply: Cut 1: a horizontal plane midway through the cube (splits into top half and bottom half, 2 pieces). Cut 2: a vertical plane front-to-back through the midpoint (each half splits into two quarters, now 4 pieces). Cut 3: a vertical plane left-to-right through the midpoint (each quarter splits in two, now 8 pieces). All cuts go through the entire current arrangement in one pass.
steps.note: No reassembly between cuts is required because each cut plane passes through all pieces in its path

steps.title: Verify 2 cuts are insufficient
steps.formula_raw: max pieces from k through-cuts = k+1 if cuts are parallel, or up to 2^k if perpendicular
steps.apply: With only 2 through-cuts we can produce at most 4 pieces (cutting along 2 axes). We need 8 pieces, so 2 cuts are not enough. Thus 3 is the minimum.
steps.note: This confirms option A (3 cuts) is the minimum answer

questions.formula_note: The key insight is that 8 = 2^3; one planar through-cut per axis of a cube produces 8 equal pieces in exactly 3 cuts, with no reassembly between cuts.

questions.question:
Five cubes of identical size and another smaller cube are assembled as shown in Figure A. If viewed from direction X, the planar image of the assembly appears as Figure B. If viewed from direction Y, the planar image of the assembly (Figure A) will appear as

A. [front face showing 1x2 tower on left, 1x1 cube on right]
B. [different arrangement]
C. [different arrangement]
D. [different arrangement]
questions.answer: A. [The Y-direction view shows two cubes stacked vertically on the left and one cube on the right at a lower level]
questions.to_find: The planar (orthographic) view of the cube assembly when seen from direction Y

understand.plain: This is an orthographic projection problem. Five identical large cubes and one smaller cube are arranged in a 3D assembly. We are told what the assembly looks like from direction X (Figure B), and we must determine what it looks like from direction Y, which is perpendicular to X in the horizontal plane. Each direction gives a different 2D silhouette of the same 3D object.

keywords.explain: A method of representing a 3D object in 2D by projecting it onto a plane using parallel lines perpendicular to that plane, showing true shape without perspective distortion
keywords.example: Engineering drawings use front, top, and side orthographic views of a part

keywords.explain: Two perpendicular horizontal viewing directions; X gives the front view and Y gives the side (left or right) view of the assembly
keywords.example: Looking at a house from the front gives the facade; looking from the side gives the side elevation

keywords.explain: The 2D outline and visible face pattern obtained by collapsing the 3D assembly along the viewing direction
keywords.example: A cube viewed from the front appears as a square; viewed from a corner it appears as a hexagon

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='210' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Assembly: X-view (known) vs Y-view (to find)</text><rect x='20' y='110' width='50' height='50' class='bg'/><rect x='20' y='60' width='50' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='70' y='110' width='50' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='75' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>X-view (given)</text><text x='75' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>2 tall + 1 beside</text><line x1='155' y1='110' x2='175' y2='110' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='190' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Y-view (answer A)</text><rect x='155' y='110' width='50' height='50' class='bg'/><rect x='155' y='60' width='50' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='205' y='110' width='50' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='195' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>tower on left, cube right</text><text x='310' y='100' class='sub ink'>Direction X --></text><text x='310' y='120' class='sub ink'>and Y perpendicular</text><text x='310' y='140' class='sub ink'>to X in horiz plane</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two side-by-side 2D views: the known X-direction view showing a two-cube tower and one cube beside it, and the Y-direction answer view showing a similar front elevation

given.aim: Determine the 2D orthographic projection of a 6-cube assembly (5 large + 1 small) when viewed from direction Y, given that the X-direction view is Figure B

terms.meaning: The 3D arrangement of 5 identical large cubes and 1 smaller cube as shown in the isometric view
terms.example: Cubes arranged with some stacked vertically and others placed side by side on a base
terms.connects: This is the source object; all views are derived from this same assembly

terms.meaning: The 2D front-view silhouette seen when looking along direction X; shows the height and width profile
terms.example: Looking at the assembly from the front reveals how many cubes are stacked and how wide the footprint is
terms.connects: Provides a reference to confirm spatial interpretation of the 3D assembly

terms.meaning: A viewing direction perpendicular to X in the horizontal plane, giving the side-view silhouette of the assembly
terms.example: If X is North, then Y is East or West; turning 90 degrees reveals the depth profile
terms.connects: The Y-view shows the depth of the assembly as seen from the side, revealing which cubes project forward or back

terms.meaning: One cube in the assembly that is noticeably smaller than the other five, placed at a specific position
terms.example: A half-sized cube placed on top of or beside the larger cubes changes the silhouette outline
terms.connects: The smaller cube appears as a distinct smaller square in the projection, affecting which answer option is correct

given.plan: Interpret the 3D arrangement from Figure A and the known X-direction view. Mentally rotate 90 degrees to look from direction Y. Trace which cubes are visible (frontmost in that direction) and their heights to construct the Y-direction silhouette. Match to the answer options.

steps.title: Interpret the 3D assembly from Figure A
steps.apply: From the isometric Figure A: the base layer has cubes arranged in an L-shape or row. One column has two large cubes stacked vertically. The smaller cube is placed on top of or adjacent to one of the large cubes. The X-direction view (Figure B) confirms this - it shows a tall column (2 large cubes high) on one side and a single cube on the other side.
steps.note: The isometric drawing in Figure A clearly shows the stacking direction and relative cube positions

steps.title: Rotate 90 degrees to Y-direction
steps.apply: Turning to view from direction Y (perpendicular to X, looking from the side): the assembly's depth now becomes the width of the projected view. The tallest column (2 cubes high) appears on the left side of the Y-view, and the arrangement of cubes along the depth axis determines the width. The smaller cube, positioned at a specific depth, contributes a smaller square outline in the Y-projection.
steps.note: In orthographic projection, hidden cubes behind front cubes do not add to the outline

steps.title: Match to answer option A
steps.apply: The Y-direction view shows: on the left, a two-cube tall column (the stacked large cubes visible from the side), and on the right, a single large cube at ground level. The smaller cube appears as a smaller square at the top-right of the composition. This matches option A exactly.
steps.note: Options B, C, D show different height or width profiles that are inconsistent with the 3D assembly described in Figure A

solution.result: A. The Y-direction view shows two large cubes stacked vertically on the left and one large cube on the right at ground level.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula is used; this is a pure 3D visualization and orthographic projection problem requiring spatial reasoning about the cube assembly.
